publications.title: The shaping of T cell receptor recognition by self-tolerance.
publications.authors: Gras S, Burrows SR, Kjer-Nielsen L, Clements CS, Liu YC, Sullivan LC, Bell MJ, Brooks AG, Purcell AW, McCluskey J, Rossjohn J.
publications.executive_summary: The study examined two archetypal T cell receptors (LC13 and CF34) recognizing HLA-B8 presenting an Epstein-Barr virus peptide (FLRGRAYGL), differing in their tolerance to a trans-inherited HLA-B44 allotype. Despite binding the same pMHC complex, the two TCRs adopted markedly different docking orientations: the alloreactive LC13 TCR, which cross-reacts with HLA-B44, engaged the C-terminal region of the HLA peptide-binding groove, whereas the CF34 TCR, selected in individuals co-inheriting HLA-B44, docked at the N-terminal region corresponding to a polymorphic difference between the two allotypes. This structural divergence demonstrates how negative selection against self-HLA allotypes directly shapes TCR specificity and recognition footprint, providing a molecular mechanism for how self-tolerance constrains the T cell repertoire while preserving pathogen responsiveness. The findings illustrate that TCR repertoire selection reflects not merely affinity thresholds but active sculpting of recognition geometry by the self-MHC environment.
